relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Abraham’s life widened again through Keturah.
More sons were born, and more peoples would branch from Abraham’s house.
The line continued through further descendants.
Midian and the sons of Keturah became part of the wider Abrahamic spread.
Yet the inheritance of the promise was given to Isaac.
The other sons received gifts and were sent eastward, away from Isaac’s covenant line.
Abraham’s years came to fullness.
He died old and full, gathered to his people.
Isaac and Ishmael came together to bury their father in Machpelah.
Abraham was laid beside Sarah in the first secured field of promise.
After Abraham’s death, blessing rested on Isaac, who dwelled by the well of the Living One who sees.
Ishmael’s generations were remembered too, the son of Hagar and Abraham.
His sons were named, beginning with his firstborn.
The line continued through more names.
The list of Ishmael’s sons came to fullness.
The twelve princes fulfilled the promise spoken over Ishmael.
Ishmael’s days ended, and he was gathered to his people.
His descendants dwelled before the face of their brothers, as the word over him had said.
The story turned to Isaac, Abraham’s son of promise.
Isaac took Rebekah from the Aramean family line.
Isaac pleaded for Rebekah, and the Eternal answered the barren place with conception.
The children struggled within her, and Rebekah sought the Eternal for meaning.
The answer came: two nations were within her, and the older would serve the younger.
When the time came, twins were in her womb.
The first came out red and hairy, and he was named Esau.
The second came grasping the heel, and he was named Jacob.
Esau became a man of field and hunt; Jacob became a quiet/complete man of tents.
The parents’ loves divided: Isaac loved Esau’s game, while Rebekah loved Jacob.
One day Jacob boiled stew, and Esau came from the field exhausted.
Esau craved the red stew, and his Edom-name was sealed in hunger.
Jacob asked for the birthright in exchange.
Esau treated the future inheritance as useless before immediate hunger.
An oath was sworn, and the birthright passed to Jacob.
Esau ate, drank, rose, and went, having treated the birthright as nothing.
